title: 11 Best Herbal Teas For Dandruff
intro: Herbal teas for dandruff are natural remedies made from various herbs and plants that help to soothe and calm the scalp, relieving symptoms of dandruff. These teas work by providing antifungal and antibacterial properties that help to control the growth of yeast on the scalp, a common cause of dandruff. Some examples of herbal teas used to treat dandruff include peppermint tea, which cools and calms the scalp, chamomile tea, which soothes and reduces inflammation, and neem tea, which has antifungal properties that help to control yeast growth. Other herbal teas such as rosemary tea, sage tea, and lavender tea can also be used to treat dandruff due to their antifungal and antibacterial properties, as well as their ability to promote healthy scalp circulation.

preparations.herb_desc: Rosmarinus officinalis teas, derived from the leaves of the rosemary plant, have been used to treat dandruff due to their anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties. The herbal preparation helps to treat this ailment by soothing the scalp, reducing flaking and itching, and promoting a healthy scalp environment. The bioactive constituents of Rosmarinus officinalis teas, including camphor, bornyl acetate, and 1,8-cineole, possess antifungal and antimicrobial activities that inhibit the growth of fungi and bacteria that contribute to dandruff. Regular consumption of Rosmarinus officinalis teas may help to alleviate dandruff symptoms, promote a balanced scalp microbiome, and improve overall scalp health.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh or dried Rosmarinus officinalis leaves and flowers.
Measure 1 tablespoon of the leaves and flowers, and place them in a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Pour 1 cup of boiling water over the Rosmarinus officinalis leaves and flowers.
Let the mixture steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain the liquid into another cup.
Drink the tea 1-2 times a day, after shampooing, to help control dandruff and soothe the scalp.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Rosmarinus officinalis teas can be used to treat dandruff, but it may cause gastrointestinal side effects such as stomach upset, nausea, and diarrhea in some individuals, especially when consumed in large quantities. When using Rosmarinus officinalis teas to treat dandruff, it's essential to start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed, and also be cautious when consuming it with other medications or substances that may interact with its active compounds.
preparation_usage: Wash hair with shampoo before using Rosmarinus officinalis tea.
Use 1 tablespoon of dried Rosmarinus officinalis leaves per cup of boiling water.
Steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the liquid.
Use the tea as a final rinse after shampooing, 2-3 times a week.
Leave the tea on hair for 1-2 minutes before rinsing with cold water.

preparation_amazon.title: Palm Beach Medicinal Herbs - Rosemary Tea - Pure Herbal Tea Series, 30ct

preparations.herb_desc: Melaleuca alternifolia teas, also known as tea tree oil tea, have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their antifungal and antibacterial properties that help to control the growth of malassezia, a fungus that contributes to this ailment. The bioactive constituents of this herbal preparation, including cineole, limonene, and terpinen-4-ol, help to soothe and calm the scalp, reducing inflammation and promoting a healthy balance of skin flora. By using Melaleuca alternifolia teas, individuals can experience the benefits of reduced flaking, itching, and redness associated with dandruff, as well as improved scalp health and a more balanced skin pH. Additionally, this herbal preparation is often used in combination with other natural ingredients to create a holistic treatment for dandruff, promoting overall well-being and a healthier scalp.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water and 1 teaspoon of dried Melaleuca alternifolia leaves.
Heat the water in a saucepan over low heat. Let it simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried Melaleuca alternifolia leaves to the water. Let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth. Discard the solids.
Let the tea cool down before applying it to your scalp. Use it as a final rinse after shampooing to help soothe dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Current topics in medicinal chemistry", Melaleuca alternifolia teas for dandruff have been reported to demonstrate anti-dandruff activity by disrupting the microbial growth associated with dandruff formation.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Melaleuca alternifolia teas can be an effective herbal remedy for treating dandruff, however, its use may cause mild side effects such as skin irritation, allergic reactions, and an increased risk of sunburn due to its high concentration of essential oils. To minimize potential side effects, it is recommended to start with a small dose, avoid using it on broken skin, and to gradually increase the amount as your body adapts, also be cautious when consuming it internally and only do so under proper preparation and guidance.
preparation_usage: Shampoo hair with normal shampoo 1-2 times a week.
Steep 1 teaspoon of Melaleuca alternifolia tea in 1 cup of hot water for 5-7 minutes.
Use tea as final rinse after shampooing 2-3 times a week.
Drink 1 cup of cooled tea 1-2 times a week as internal treatment.
Repeat treatment for 2-3 months for noticeable dandruff reduction.

preparations.herb_desc: Cymbopogon citratus teas, also known as lemongrass tea, have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their antifungal and antibacterial properties, which help to control the growth of pathogens that contribute to the development of this condition. The bioactive constituents of this herbal preparation, such as citral and geraniol, have been found to exhibit antifungal activity against Malassezia, a fungus that is commonly associated with dandruff. By reducing the proliferation of this fungus, Cymbopogon citratus teas help to alleviate symptoms of dandruff, including flaking and itching, thereby promoting a healthy scalp and hair growth. The benefits of using Cymbopogon citratus teas to treat dandruff include their natural and non-toxic composition, which makes them a safer alternative to conventional treatments, as well as their potential to reduce inflammation and promote a balanced skin pH.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Cymbopogon citratus leaves and 1 cup of water.
Steep the leaves in the water for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the mixture and discard the leaves.
Add 2 tablespoons of lemon juice to the tea and mix well.
Use the tea as a final rinse after shampooing your hair to help treat dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Cymbopogon citratus teas can be used to treat dandruff, but it may cause side effects such as skin irritation, allergic reactions, and an increased risk of liver damage in some individuals. To use Cymbopogon citratus teas safely, it's essential to start with small amounts, monitor your body's response, and avoid consuming it for extended periods, as prolonged use may lead to stomach upset and interact with other medications.
preparation_usage: Wet your hair with warm water before applying the tea.
Apply 2-3 tablespoons of Cymbopogon citratus tea to your scalp.
Massage the tea into your scalp for 5-7 minutes.
Leave the tea on for 30 minutes to an hour before rinsing.
Repeat twice a week for 2-3 weeks to see noticeable results.

preparations.herb_desc: Eucalyptus globulus teas, derived from the leaves of the eucalyptus tree, have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their antifungal and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents, including cineole, camphor, and limonene, help to reduce the growth of fungi that contribute to dandruff, while also soothing and calming the scalp. By reducing inflammation and promoting a healthy scalp environment, eucalyptus globulus teas help to alleviate symptoms of dandruff, including itchiness and flakiness. The benefits of using eucalyptus globulus teas to treat dandruff include natural and chemical-free relief, reduced risk of scalp irritation, and a soothing and calming effect on the scalp.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Eucalyptus globulus leaves or 1 teaspoon of dried leaves.
Boil 1 cup of water in a pot.
Add the Eucalyptus leaves to the boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup and discard the leaves.
Let the tea cool down and use it as a final rinse after shampooing your hair to help treat dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Current topics in medicinal chemistry", Eucalyptus globulus teas for dandruff have been reported to demonstrate anti-dandruff activity by disrupting the microbial growth associated with dandruff formation.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Eucalyptus globulus teas can be used to treat dandruff due to its antifungal and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help to reduce flaking and soothe the scalp. However, potential side effects of using Eucalyptus globulus teas to treat dandruff include digestive issues, allergic reactions, and increased heart rate, and precautions should be taken to avoid using excessive amounts, as it may cause interactions with other medications and worsen conditions such as asthma and kidney problems.
preparation_usage: Use 1/2 cup of Eucalyptus globulus tea as final rinse after shampooing.
Apply tea to scalp, massaging for 2-3 minutes before rinsing.
Leave the tea on scalp for 5-7 minutes before rinsing thoroughly.
Repeat the process 2-3 times a week for best results.
Use 1 teaspoon of Eucalyptus globulus powder as shampoo substitute once a week.

preparation_amazon.title: Jovvily Eucalyptus Leaves - 1lb - Dried - Cut & Sifted - Herbal Tea

preparations.herb_desc: Aloe barbadensis teas have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties, which help to soothe and calm the scalp. The herbal preparation works by reducing inflammation, preventing fungal growth, and promoting a healthy scalp environment, ultimately leading to reduced dandruff symptoms. Aloe barbadensis teas contain bioactive constituents such as aloin, aloe-emodin, and acemannan, which exhibit antiseptic, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties that help to treat dandruff. The benefits of using aloe barbadensis teas for dandruff treatment include reduced flaking, itching, and redness, as well as improved scalp health and a natural, chemical-free solution for this common scalp condition.
preparation_procedure: Cut 2 inches of Aloe barbadensis leaf from the plant, making sure it's free of any yellow spots.
Grind the leaf into a pulp using a spoon or a grinder.
Mix 1 cup of water with 2 tablespoons of the aloe pulp in a saucepan.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the liquid and drink 1 cup of the aloe tea twice a day to help soothe dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Current topics in medicinal chemistry", Aloe barbadensis teas for dandruff have been reported to demonstrate anti-dandruff activity by disrupting the microbial growth associated with dandruff formation.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Aloe barbadensis teas can be used to treat dandruff, but it may cause side effects such as stomach upset, allergic reactions, and skin irritation in some individuals, particularly those with sensitive skin. To use Aloe barbadensis teas effectively and safely, it is recommended to start with a small dose, avoid taking it in large quantities, and be cautious when using it in combination with other medications or skin treatments.
preparation_usage: Wash your hair with a mild shampoo to clean scalp.
Apply 2 tablespoons of Aloe barbadensis tea to scalp.
Massage tea into scalp for 2-3 minutes to stimulate blood flow.
Leave the tea on for 30 minutes to an hour before shampooing.
Repeat application 2-3 times a week for best results.

preparation_amazon.title: The Republic of Tea Beautifying Botanicals® Clean Beauty Berry Aloe Herbal Tea Bags (36 count)

preparations.herb_desc: Zingiber officinale teas, derived from the rhizome of the ginger plant, have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties. The bioactive constituents of ginger, such as gingerol and shogaol, exhibit potent antifungal activity against Malassezia, a yeast that contributes to dandruff development. By inhibiting the growth of this yeast, ginger tea helps to reduce flaking and itching associated with dandruff, promoting a healthy scalp and scalp skin. Regular consumption of ginger tea has been shown to provide relief from dandruff symptoms, making it a beneficial herbal preparation for managing this common scalp condition.
preparation_procedure: Boil 1 cup of water in a pot.
Add 2-3 slices of fresh Zingiber officinale (about 2 tablespoons) to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup and let it cool.
Use the tea as a final rinse after shampooing to help soothe and prevent dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Zingiber officinale teas can be used to treat dandruff due to their antifungal and anti-inflammatory properties, but some possible side effects include gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, and increased heart rate in individuals with pre-existing heart conditions. Precautions to take when using Zingiber officinale teas to treat dandruff include monitoring for signs of allergic reactions, such as hives or difficulty breathing, and starting with low doses to assess tolerance and adjust accordingly.
preparation_usage: Use 1 teaspoon of dried Zingiber officinale root powder per cup of boiling water.
Steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the mixture into a clean cup.
Drink 1-2 cups of the tea 2-3 times a week to help control dandruff.
Leave the tea on your scalp for 10-15 minutes before washing your hair.
Maintain a healthy scalp by washing your hair regularly and eating a balanced diet.

preparation_amazon.title: FGO Organic Ginger Tea, 100 Count, Eco-Conscious Tea Bags, Caffeine Free, Packaging May Vary (Pack of 1)

preparations.herb_desc: Lavandula angustifolia teas, derived from the fragrant flowers of the lavender plant, have been used to treat dandruff due to their antifungal and anti-inflammatory properties. This herbal preparation helps to treat dandruff by reducing the growth of Malassezia yeast, a common cause of the condition, and soothing irritated scalps. The bioactive constituents of Lavandula angustifolia teas, including linalool and linalyl acetate, have been shown to possess antifungal and antimicrobial properties that help to combat dandruff-causing microorganisms. Regular consumption of Lavandula angustifolia teas has been found to provide relief from dandruff symptoms, promoting a healthy scalp and reducing flaking.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried Lavandula angustifolia flowers.
Boil 1 cup of water and let it cool for 5 minutes.
Add the dried flowers to the cooled water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture and discard the flowers.
Use the tea as a final rinse after shampooing to help soothe and calm dandruff-prone scalp.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Lavandula angustifolia teas can be a potential remedy for treating dandruff, but it may cause side effects such as skin irritation, allergic reactions, or an increased risk of bleeding due to its anticoagulant properties. To use Lavandula angustifolia teas safely, it's essential to start with a small dose and gradually increase it to minimize the risk of skin sensitivity and other adverse reactions.
preparation_usage: Wash your hair with a mild shampoo to remove dirt and impurities.
Steep 1 tablespoon of Lavandula angustifolia tea in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Let the tea cool down to room temperature before using it as a final rinse.
Use the cooled tea as a final rinse after shampooing and conditioner once a week.
Repeat the process for 2-3 weeks to see noticeable reduction in dandruff.

preparations.herb_desc: Ginkgo biloba teas have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties, which help to soothe and calm the scalp. The bioactive constituents of Ginkgo biloba, including flavonoids and terpenoids, have been shown to reduce inflammation and prevent the growth of fungal organisms that can contribute to dandruff. By reducing inflammation and preventing fungal growth, Ginkgo biloba teas can help to alleviate symptoms of dandruff, such as flaking and itchiness, and promote a healthy scalp. The benefits of using Ginkgo biloba teas to treat dandruff include a reduction in symptoms, improved scalp health, and a natural and non-invasive approach to managing this common condition.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water and 1 teaspoon of dried Ginkgo biloba leaves.
Boil the water and let it cool for 5 minutes.
Add the Ginkgo biloba leaves to a tea infuser or a small muslin bag.
Steep the leaves in the water for 5-7 minutes, then remove the leaves.
Strain the tea into a cup and drink it 2-3 times a week to help with dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Ginkgo biloba teas can be used to treat dandruff, but it may cause side effects such as dizziness, stomach upset, and allergic reactions in some individuals. Precautions should be taken when using Ginkgo biloba teas to treat dandruff, including avoiding it if you have bleeding disorders, taking it with food to minimize stomach upset, and monitoring your body's reaction to the herbal preparation.
preparation_usage: Consult a doctor before using Ginkgo biloba tea for dandruff treatment.
Use 1-2 teaspoons of dried Ginkgo biloba leaves per cup of boiling water.
Steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain the tea.
Drink 1-2 cups of Ginkgo biloba tea 2-3 times a week for dandruff relief.
Shampoo hair as usual after drinking the tea for best results.

preparations.herb_desc: Curcuma longa teas have been gaining attention for their potential in treating dandruff, a common scalp condition characterized by flaky skin and itchiness. The anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties of these teas, which are attributed to the presence of curcuminoids and other bioactive constituents, help to soothe and calm the scalp, thereby reducing inflammation and preventing the growth of fungi that can contribute to dandruff. The bioactive constituents of Curcuma longa teas, including curcumin, demethoxycurcumin, and bisdemethoxycurcumin, possess potent antioxidant and antimicrobial properties that help to eliminate the root causes of dandruff, leading to improved scalp health and reduced flaking. By incorporating Curcuma longa teas into one's hair care routine, individuals can reap the benefits of a healthy scalp, including reduced dandruff, improved hair growth, and a decrease in scalp irritation.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried Curcuma longa root and 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the Curcuma longa root in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup and discard the solids.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey or sugar (optional) to the tea and mix well.
Drink the tea once a day, preferably before bedtime, for 1-2 weeks for relief from dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Curcuma longa teas can be an effective treatment for dandruff due to its antifungal and antibacterial properties, but possible side effects include skin irritation, allergic reactions, and increased sensitivity to sunlight. Precautions to take when using Curcuma longa teas include starting with small amounts to gauge tolerance, avoiding direct skin contact, and rinsing the hair thoroughly after treatment to minimize potential side effects.
preparation_usage: Boil 1 cup of water for 5-7 minutes.
Steep 1 teaspoon of Curcuma longa tea in boiling water for 5 minutes.
Strain and let the tea cool down to room temperature.
Apply the cooled tea as a final rinse after shampooing 2-3 times a week.
Use 2-3 tablespoons of the tea as a leave-in treatment 1-2 times a week.

preparations.herb_desc: Urtica dioica teas, also known as nettle leaf tea, have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their anti-inflammatory, antifungal, and antibacterial properties. The herbal preparation helps to treat dandruff by reducing inflammation and preventing the overgrowth of yeast, a common cause of this condition. The bioactive constituents of Urtica dioica, including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, contribute to its therapeutic effects by reducing oxidative stress and modulating the immune response. Drinking Urtica dioica teas regularly can provide benefits in treating dandruff, including reduced flaking, improved scalp health, and a decrease in the frequency of flare-ups.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Urtica dioica leaves and flowers, or 2 tablespoons of dried leaves and flowers.
Add 1 cup of boiling water to the Urtica dioica leaves and flowers in a saucepan.
Let the mixture steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain it using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve.
Combine the strained liquid with 1 cup of cold water, and add 1 teaspoon of apple cider vinegar (optional).
Store the Urtica dioica tea in a glass bottle in the refrigerator for up to 3 days. Drink 1/2 cup of the tea 2-3 times a day to help with dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Urtica dioica teas can be used to treat dandruff, but possible side effects may include skin irritation, allergic reactions, and stomach upset due to its potential to interact with other medications or worsen digestive issues. Precautions should be taken when using this herbal preparation, such as starting with small doses, avoiding use on broken skin, and being aware of any potential allergic reactions, especially if you have sensitive skin.
preparation_usage: Wash hair with shampoo before using Urtica dioica tea.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of dried Urtica dioica leaves in 1 cup boiling water.
Let the tea cool and use it as final rinse after shampooing.
Use 1/4 cup of tea as final rinse, 2-3 times a week.
Continue for 2-4 weeks to see improvements in dandruff.

preparations.herb_desc: Salvia officinalis teas, derived from the leaves of the sage plant, have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties. The herbal preparation helps to treat dandruff by reducing scalp irritation, promoting a healthy scalp environment, and controlling the growth of fungus that can contribute to the condition. Bioactive constituents such as triterpenoids, phenolic acids, and rosmarinic acid present in Salvia officinalis teas have been found to exhibit antifungal and anti-inflammatory activities, which are beneficial in treating dandruff. The benefits of using Salvia officinalis teas to treat dandruff include a reduction in flaking, itching, and redness of the scalp, promoting a healthy and balanced scalp ecosystem.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Salvia officinalis leaves or 2 tablespoons of dried leaves.
Boil 1 cup of water in a pot.
Add the Salvia officinalis leaves to the boiling water, then reduce heat to low.
Let the mixture steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain it.
Let the tea cool down, then apply it to the scalp to help with dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Salvia officinalis teas can be used to treat dandruff due to their antifungal and antibacterial properties, but possible side effects include skin irritation, allergic reactions, and gastrointestinal issues such as nausea and stomach upset. To minimize potential side effects, it is recommended to start with small amounts, avoid consuming the teas in excess, and monitor your skin condition closely for any adverse reactions.
preparation_usage: Steep 1 teaspoon of dried Salvia officinalis leaves in 1 cup of boiling water.
Strain the mixture and let it cool to room temperature.
Use this cooled mixture as a final rinse after shampooing 2-3 times a week.
Apply the mixture to the scalp and massage for 5 minutes before rinsing.
Repeat the process for 2-4 weeks to see noticeable improvements in dandruff.

preparations.herb_desc: Saponaria officinalis teas are a natural remedy used to treat dandruff, a scalp condition characterized by flakes and itching. The anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties of Saponaria officinalis teas help to soothe and calm the scalp, reducing inflammation and preventing fungal growth that contributes to dandruff. The bioactive constituents, including saponins and flavonoids, exhibit antimicrobial activity, inhibiting the growth of pathogenic microorganisms that cause dandruff. Regular consumption of Saponaria officinalis teas may help to alleviate symptoms of dandruff, promoting a healthy scalp and reducing the recurrence of flakes and itching.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Saponaria officinalis leaves and flowers, or 1/2 cup of dried ones.
Combine 1 cup of boiling water with 1-2 tablespoons of the gathered Saponaria officinalis.
Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes, or until it reaches your desired tea strength.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the solids. Add honey or lemon if desired.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a week to help reduce dandruff symptoms. Store any leftover tea in the fridge.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Saponaria officinalis teas can be used as a natural remedy to treat dandruff, but possible side effects may include skin irritation, allergic reactions, and stomach upset in some individuals. To minimize these risks, it is recommended to start with a small dose, avoid using it if you have sensitive skin, and monitor your body's response closely for any adverse reactions.

preparations.herb_desc: Equisetum arvense teas have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to its anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties, which help soothe and calm the scalp, reducing flaking and itching. The bioactive constituents present in this herbal preparation, including flavonoids, saponins, and alkaloids, have been shown to inhibit the growth of pathogenic fungi that contribute to dandruff. By reducing inflammation and inhibiting fungal growth, Equisetum arvense teas help to treat dandruff by promoting a healthy scalp environment and preventing further irritation. Regular consumption of Equisetum arvense teas has been found to provide relief from dandruff symptoms, promoting a clear and healthy scalp.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Equisetum arvense roots and 4 cups of water in a pot.
Bring the water to a boil, then add the dried roots and reduce heat to low.
Simmer for 10 minutes, then turn off the heat and let it steep for 30 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup and discard the roots. Add 1 tablespoon of honey for taste.
Drink 1/2 cup of the tea 2 times a day to help treat dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Equisetum arvense teas can be used to treat dandruff, but possible side effects may include digestive issues, allergic reactions, or interactions with other medications due to its anti-inflammatory properties. When using Equisetum arvense teas to treat dandruff, it is recommended to start with low doses and gradually increase as needed, and to avoid using it for extended periods without proper monitoring of its effects on the body.

preparations.herb_desc: Cassia auriculata teas have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their anti-inflammatory, antifungal, and antibacterial properties. The herbal preparation helps to treat dandruff by reducing inflammation and soothing the scalp, thus providing relief from itching and flaking. The bioactive constituents, including flavonoids, terpenoids, and saponins, in Cassia auriculata teas have been found to possess antimicrobial properties that help to control the growth of fungi and bacteria that cause dandruff. The benefits of using Cassia auriculata teas to treat dandruff include reduced inflammation, improved scalp health, and a decrease in the frequency and severity of dandruff episodes.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of Cassia auriculata flowers and 1 cup of water.
Boil the water in a pot and add the Cassia auriculata flowers.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, then strain the flowers.
Mix 1 teaspoon of the tea with 1 cup of water to make the final tea.
Use the tea as a final rinse after shampooing your hair to help soothe dandruff.

To use Cassia auriculata teas safely, it is recommended to start with a small dose, avoid consuming it for extended periods, and monitor your body's response to the herbal preparation.

preparations.herb_desc: Thymus serpyllum teas, derived from the herb thyme, have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to its antifungal, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of thyme, including thymol and carvacrol, help to soothe and balance the scalp, reducing flaking and itching associated with dandruff. By inhibiting the growth of Malassezia, a fungus that contributes to dandruff, Thymus serpyllum teas promote a healthy scalp environment and alleviate symptoms of the condition. Regular consumption of Thymus serpyllum teas may lead to reduced dandruff occurrence, improved scalp health, and a more balanced skin pH.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Thymus serpyllum leaves. You can find them in health food stores or grow them yourself.
Dry the leaves in a low-temperature oven (150°F - 200°F) for 30 minutes or air-dry them for 2-3 days.
Measure 1 tablespoon of dried Thymus serpyllum leaves and place them in a tea infuser or a small muslin bag.
Steep the leaves in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes. Let it cool for 2-3 minutes.
Strain the tea and use it as a final rinse after shampooing your hair to help treat dandruff.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Current topics in medicinal chemistry, Thymus serpyllum teas for dandruff may exhibit anti-dandruff activity by disrupting microbial growth associated with dandruff formation, similar to other alternative therapies such as tea tree oil and thyme.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Thymus serpyllum teas can be used to treat dandruff due to its antifungal and antibacterial properties, but it may cause side effects such as skin irritation, allergic reactions, or digestive issues in some individuals. To use Thymus serpyllum teas safely, take precautions by starting with small amounts, avoiding it if you have sensitive skin or allergies, and monitoring your body's response to the herbal preparation to minimize potential side effects.

preparations.herb_desc: Camellia sinensis teas, derived from the leaves of the tea plant, have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their antifungal, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory properties. The herbal preparation's ability to balance the scalp's pH and reduce inflammation helps to alleviate dandruff symptoms, promoting a healthy scalp environment. The bioactive constituents of Camellia sinensis teas, including catechins, theaflavins, and thearubigins, help to inhibit the growth of Malassezia yeast, a common contributor to dandruff. Regular consumption of Camellia sinensis teas can provide benefits in treating dandruff, including reduced flaking, itching, and inflammation, as well as a balanced scalp microbiome.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried Camellia sinensis leaves and 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the leaves in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a bowl and let it cool.
Mix 1 tablespoon of apple cider vinegar and 1 tablespoon of honey into the cooled tea.
Use the tea as a final rinse after shampooing your hair, 2-3 times a week for best results.

preparations.herb_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas have been traditionally used to treat dandruff, a common scalp condition characterized by flaking skin and inflammation. The antifungal and anti-inflammatory properties of this herbal preparation help to soothe the scalp, reducing the symptoms of dandruff. The bioactive constituents of Glycyrrhiza glabra teas, including glycyrrhizin and flavonoids, have been found to exhibit potent antifungal and antioxidant activities, which contribute to its effectiveness in treating dandruff. Regular consumption of Glycyrrhiza glabra teas has been found to promote healthy scalp conditions, reduce dandruff, and provide a soothing and calming effect on the scalp.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water and 1 tablespoon of dried Glycyrrhiza glabra root.
Boil the water in a pot, then add the dried root.
Let it simmer for 5-7 minutes, then turn off the heat.
Strain the liquid into a cup and discard the root.
Drink the tea twice a day to help with dandruff treatment.

Precautions when using Glycyrrhiza glabra teas to treat dandruff include limiting the dosage and frequency of consumption, avoiding use in individuals with kidney or liver issues, and monitoring for any adverse reactions, as excessive consumption can lead to hormonal imbalances and other complications.

preparations.herb_desc: Aloe vera teas have been traditionally used to treat dandruff, a common scalp condition characterized by flaking skin and itchiness. The anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties of aloe vera help to soothe and calm the scalp, reducing irritation and inflammation. The bioactive constituents, including aloin, aloe-emodin, and acemannan, possess anti-inflammatory and antifungal activities that help to control the growth of fungi and bacteria that contribute to dandruff. By using aloe vera teas, individuals can benefit from its soothing and antifungal properties, which can help to reduce flaking, itching, and inflammation associated with dandruff, promoting a healthy and balanced scalp.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water, 1/2 cup of aloe vera gel, and 1 tablespoon of honey in a saucepan.
Heat the water over low heat and add the aloe vera gel. Stir until the gel is fully dissolved.
Remove the saucepan from heat and let it cool for a few minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup and add the honey. Stir until the honey is fully dissolved.
Drink the aloe vera tea once a day to help soothe dandruff and promote healthy scalp.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Current topics in medicinal chemistry, Aloe vera teas for dandruff have been reported to demonstrate anti-dandruff activity by disrupting the microbial growth associated with dandruff formation.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Aloe vera teas can be used to treat dandruff, but some possible side effects may include digestive issues, allergic reactions, or an increased risk of skin irritation due to the high concentration of aloe sap. To minimize these risks, it's recommended to start with a small dose, gradually increasing the amount as needed, and to avoid consuming aloe vera teas if you have sensitive stomach or allergies.

preparations.herb_desc: Achillea millefolium teas have been traditionally used to treat dandruff due to their anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties. preparations.herb_desc: Thymus vulgaris teas have been used for centuries to treat various health issues, including dandruff, due to its antifungal and antiseptic properties. This herbal preparation helps to treat dandruff by reducing inflammation, preventing fungal growth, and promoting a healthy scalp. The bioactive constituents of Thymus vulgaris teas, such as carvacrol and thymol, exhibit antimicrobial activity that helps to combat the fungal infection causing dandruff. The benefits of using Thymus vulgaris teas to treat dandruff include reduced flaking, itchiness, and redness, promoting a clear and healthy scalp.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of boiling water, 1 tablespoon of dried thyme leaves (Thymus vulgaris), and a teapot.
Add the thyme leaves to the teapot and pour in the boiling water.
Steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer or cheesecloth.
Add 1 tablespoon of coconut oil or olive oil to the tea and mix well.
Use the tea as a final rinse after shampooing your hair, 1-2 times a week, to help soothe and treat dandruff.

preparations.preparation_side_effect: Thymus vulgaris teas can help alleviate dandruff symptoms, but possible side effects may include skin irritation, allergic reactions, and increased heart rate, particularly when consumed in large quantities. To use Thymus vulgaris teas safely, take the recommended dosage, avoid consumption if you have sensitive skin or are prone to allergic reactions, and monitor your body's response to the herbal preparation.
